does: This paper addresses machine unlearning by decoupling class labels from target concepts, moving beyond conventional class-wise forgetting methods. The authors identify and formalize three new forgetting scenarios: target mismatch (where the concept to forget differs from the class label), model mismatch (forgetting across different model architectures), and data mismatch (forgetting specific instances versus entire classes). They conduct systematic analysis of forgetting dynamics at the representation level and propose TARF (TARget-aware Forgetting), a general framework that enables selective forgetting of specific concepts while preserving model utility on retained data. The framework addresses the practical gap where class labels and underlying concepts don't align, providing methods to precisely target conceptual knowledge rather than crude class-level removal.
connects: This work builds on the growing machine unlearning literature, which has emerged from data privacy regulations like GDPR requiring models to 'forget' training data. Previous approaches primarily focused on class-wise unlearning through gradient ascent on forgetting data or fine-tuning on retained data, treating the class label as the primary unit of forgetting. This paper extends those foundations by recognizing limitations in existing frameworks that assume label-concept alignment. It connects to broader research on concept-based learning, representation analysis in neural networks, and fine-grained control over model behavior. The decoupling perspective relates to work on disentangled representations and the understanding of what knowledge neural networks encode at different layers.
enables: This framework opens directions for more nuanced privacy-preserving machine learning where practitioners can target specific concepts or knowledge rather than entire classes. Future work could explore forgetting fine-grained concepts within classes, handling concept drift during unlearning, or extending to multimodal models where label-concept misalignment is even more prevalent. The representation-level analysis suggests pathways for interpretability research and could enable adversarial robustness techniques that remove specific knowledge patterns. The decoupling perspective may also inform federated learning and continual learning scenarios where selective forgetting of outdated or sensitive information is valuable, and could support emerging applications in responsible AI where granular control over model knowledge is legally or ethically required.
